questions.scenario: A company's GenAI chatbot processes customer inquiries with varying complexity. Analysis shows that 70% of queries are simple FAQs that could be answered by a smaller, less expensive model, while 30% require more sophisticated reasoning. The team wants to reduce costs without degrading quality for complex queries.
questions.question: Which cost optimization strategy BEST addresses this variable query complexity?

options.text: Use the most capable model for all queries to ensure consistent quality
Implement model cascading: route simple queries to Claude Haiku and complex queries to Claude Sonnet
Fine-tune a single model to handle both simple and complex queries efficiently
Batch all queries and process them during off-peak hours for lower pricing

questions.explanation: Model cascading routes queries to appropriate models based on complexity. Simple queries go to cheaper models (Haiku), complex queries go to capable models (Sonnet). This optimizes cost while maintaining quality where it matters.

incorrectExplanations.a: Using the most capable model for all queries wastes money on simple queries that don't need sophisticated reasoning.
incorrectExplanations.c: Fine-tuning adds cost and doesn't address the fundamental price difference between model tiers.
incorrectExplanations.d: Batching doesn't address model selection. You'd still pay the same per-token rates regardless of when processing occurs.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Variable complexity = model cascading (route to appropriate tier)

questions.examTip: Model cascading is the answer for cost optimization with variable query complexity. Route simple queries to cheaper models.

questions.scenario: A customer support application receives many similar queries about product features. Analysis shows that 40% of queries are paraphrased versions of the same questions. The team wants to reduce costs by avoiding redundant model invocations.
questions.question: Which caching strategy handles paraphrased queries effectively?

options.text: Use CloudFront caching with exact URL matching
Implement semantic caching using embedding similarity to match semantically similar queries
Cache responses in DynamoDB with query text as the primary key
Use Bedrock prompt caching to store the system prompt prefix

questions.explanation: Semantic caching uses embedding similarity to identify queries with the same meaning, even if worded differently. 'What's the return policy?' and 'How do I return items?' would match in a semantic cache but not in exact-match caches.

incorrectExplanations.a: CloudFront exact matching only works for identical requests. Paraphrased queries have different URLs/request bodies.
incorrectExplanations.c: DynamoDB with query text key requires exact matches. Paraphrases won't hit the cache.
incorrectExplanations.d: Prompt caching saves processing of system prompts but doesn't help with repeated user queries. Different use case.

eliminationHints: Query text key = exact match only
Prompt caching = system prompts, not queries
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Paraphrased query caching = semantic caching with embeddings

questions.examTip: Semantic caching matches queries by meaning, not exact text. Use it when queries may be paraphrased.

questions.scenario: A content generation application produces long-form articles (2000+ words). Users complain that they have to wait 30+ seconds to see any content. The team wants to improve perceived responsiveness without changing the content quality.
questions.question: Which technique provides the BEST improvement in perceived latency for long-form generation?

options.text: Increase Lambda memory to speed up processing
Use streaming with InvokeModelWithResponseStream to show content as it generates
Pre-generate common articles during off-peak hours
Use a larger model that generates faster

questions.explanation: Streaming displays content as it generates, showing users progress immediately. For long-form content, users see the first words within seconds while the rest generates. This dramatically improves perceived latency even though total generation time is similar.

incorrectExplanations.a: Lambda memory affects compute but has minimal impact on FM inference time. The bottleneck is sequential token generation.
incorrectExplanations.c: Pre-generation works for predictable content but not for user-requested articles on variable topics.
incorrectExplanations.d: Larger models generally generate slower, not faster. Model size correlates with capability, not speed.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Perceived latency for long generation = streaming

questions.examTip: Streaming improves perceived latency. Users see progress immediately instead of waiting for complete generation.

questions.scenario: A RAG application's retrieval step is taking 3 seconds on average, which is 80% of total request latency. The vector database uses Amazon OpenSearch Service with 10 million documents. The team needs to reduce retrieval latency.
questions.question: Which combination of optimizations MOST effectively reduces retrieval latency?

options.text: Increase OpenSearch instance size and enable caching for repeated queries
Optimize shard count, reduce the number of returned results, and tune the ANN algorithm parameters
Migrate to DynamoDB for faster key-value lookups
Add more replicas to distribute the query load

questions.explanation: Vector search latency depends on index configuration (shard count), result set size (fewer results = faster), and ANN algorithm tuning (ef_search parameter trades accuracy for speed). These directly address search performance.

incorrectExplanations.a: Larger instances help but don't address the fundamental search configuration. Caching only helps for repeated identical queries.
incorrectExplanations.c: DynamoDB doesn't support vector similarity search. It's for key-value, not semantic retrieval.
incorrectExplanations.d: More replicas improve availability and can help with read throughput but don't reduce individual query latency.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Vector retrieval latency = index tuning + result count + ANN parameters

questions.examTip: Retrieval latency optimization involves index configuration (shards), result count, and ANN algorithm parameters.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application spans multiple AWS services: API Gateway, Lambda, Bedrock, and a Knowledge Base. Users report intermittent slow responses, but CloudWatch metrics don't clearly show which component is causing delays.
questions.question: Which AWS service enables identification of latency bottlenecks across the request path?

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch Logs with log group queries
AWS X-Ray for distributed tracing across services
Amazon CloudWatch Metrics with detailed monitoring
AWS CloudTrail for API call analysis

questions.explanation: X-Ray provides distributed tracing, showing the time spent in each service component of a request. You can visualize the entire request path and identify which service is causing latency—whether it's API Gateway, Lambda cold start, Bedrock inference, or Knowledge Base retrieval.

incorrectExplanations.a: CloudWatch Logs capture text output but don't provide cross-service timing visualization. You'd need to manually correlate timestamps.
incorrectExplanations.c: CloudWatch Metrics show aggregate statistics per service but don't trace individual requests across services.
incorrectExplanations.d: CloudTrail logs API calls for audit purposes, not performance analysis. It shows what was called, not how long it took.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: latency bottlenecks across the request path
eliminationHints: CloudWatch Logs = text, not timing visualization
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Distributed latency analysis = X-Ray tracing

questions.examTip: X-Ray traces requests across services. Use it to identify bottlenecks in distributed GenAI applications.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application team needs to track token usage, response quality scores, and guardrail trigger rates for cost management and quality monitoring. These are custom metrics not natively provided by AWS services.
questions.question: How should the team implement custom GenAI-specific monitoring?

options.text: Parse Bedrock invocation logs and generate reports manually
Publish custom CloudWatch metrics from the application code with dimensions for segmentation
Store metrics in DynamoDB and query with custom dashboards
Use Amazon QuickSight to analyze Bedrock usage data

questions.explanation: Custom CloudWatch metrics allow you to track GenAI-specific measurements. Dimensions enable segmentation by application, feature, or user segment. CloudWatch provides dashboards, alarms, and anomaly detection on these custom metrics.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual log parsing doesn't scale and doesn't provide real-time visibility or alerting.
incorrectExplanations.c: Custom DynamoDB metrics storage reinvents CloudWatch. It adds complexity without providing CloudWatch's built-in capabilities.
incorrectExplanations.d: QuickSight is for business intelligence dashboards, not operational monitoring with real-time alerting.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Custom operational metrics = CloudWatch custom metrics

questions.examTip: Custom CloudWatch metrics handle GenAI-specific measurements. Use dimensions for segmentation and analysis.

questions.scenario: A company runs a GenAI application with predictable daily traffic. During business hours, they consistently process 50,000 tokens per minute. After hours, usage drops to nearly zero. They currently use on-demand Bedrock pricing and want to optimize costs.
questions.question: Which capacity configuration provides the MOST cost-effective pricing for this usage pattern?

options.text: Continue with on-demand pricing for flexibility
Purchase Provisioned Throughput for peak capacity (50,000 tokens/min)
Purchase Provisioned Throughput sized for average daily usage
Use a combination of Provisioned Throughput for business hours baseline plus on-demand for overflow

questions.explanation: Provisioned Throughput provides lower per-token costs but requires commitment. A hybrid approach uses provisioned capacity during predictable business hours and avoids paying for unused capacity overnight. This optimizes costs across the usage pattern.

incorrectExplanations.a: On-demand is more expensive per token. With 50K tokens/min sustained usage, provisioned would save significant costs.
incorrectExplanations.b: Provisioning for peak wastes money during zero-usage off-hours. You'd pay for capacity that sits unused.
incorrectExplanations.c: Average daily usage doesn't account for the zero-usage off-hours. This would still overprovision for the usage pattern.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: predictable daily traffic with off-hours drop
eliminationHints: On-demand only = expensive for sustained usage
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Predictable business-hours usage = Provisioned for baseline + on-demand for flexibility

questions.examTip: Provisioned Throughput saves money at sustained usage. Consider usage patterns to size appropriately.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application has been running for several months. Users report that answer quality has degraded recently, but there have been no code changes. The team suspects model drift or data quality issues.
questions.question: Which approach BEST detects and diagnoses quality degradation?

options.text: Review recent user complaints and manually test reported issues
Run Bedrock Model Evaluations against a golden dataset and compare to historical baselines
Increase monitoring of CloudWatch error rates
Analyze X-Ray traces for latency changes

questions.explanation: Golden datasets with expected outputs enable systematic quality testing. Running evaluations against these datasets and comparing to historical results reveals quality drift. If scores decline, you can investigate specific failure cases.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual complaint review doesn't systematically measure quality or provide comparable baselines.
incorrectExplanations.c: Error rates measure technical failures, not quality degradation. The model might return 'incorrect' answers without throwing errors.
incorrectExplanations.d: X-Ray measures latency, not output quality. Quality degradation may have no latency correlation.

eliminationHints: Error rates = technical failures, not quality
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Quality drift detection = golden dataset evaluation with baseline comparison

questions.examTip: Golden datasets enable quality regression testing. Compare current results to historical baselines to detect drift.

questions.scenario: A developer notices their Bedrock API costs are higher than expected. Each request includes a 2000-token system prompt, but actual user queries average only 50 tokens.
questions.question: Which feature reduces the cost of repeatedly processing the same system prompt?

options.text: Use a smaller model for system prompt processing
Enable Bedrock prompt caching to reuse processed system prompts
Move system prompt instructions to the user message
Reduce the system prompt length by half

questions.explanation: Prompt caching stores processed system prompts, reducing the per-request cost of processing the same instructions repeatedly. With a 2000-token system prompt and 50-token queries, caching can provide significant savings.

incorrectExplanations.a: You can't use different models for different parts of a single prompt. The entire request goes to one model.
incorrectExplanations.c: Moving to user message doesn't reduce token count or processing. It just reorganizes the prompt.
incorrectExplanations.d: Reducing prompt length might hurt quality. Prompt caching preserves full functionality while reducing cost.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: repeatedly processing the same system prompt
eliminationHints: Move to user message = same tokens
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Repeated system prompt = prompt caching

questions.examTip: Prompt caching reduces cost for repeated system prompts. Think prompt caching when you see large static prefixes.

questions.scenario: A creative writing application uses GenAI to generate stories. Users want varied, imaginative outputs. However, some users find the outputs too similar across multiple requests.
questions.question: Which parameter should be adjusted to increase output variety?

options.text: Increase the temperature parameter
Increase max_tokens
Change to a different model
Add more context to the prompt

questions.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness in token selection, leading to more varied and creative outputs. For creative writing, temperatures of 0.7-1.0 produce diverse results.

incorrectExplanations.b: max_tokens controls length, not variety. Longer outputs aren't inherently more varied.
incorrectExplanations.c: Model changes affect capabilities but aren't the primary control for output variety.
incorrectExplanations.d: More context guides content but doesn't increase randomness in expression.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Output variety/creativity = higher temperature

questions.examTip: Temperature controls creativity/randomness. Higher = more varied, lower = more consistent.

questions.scenario: A company runs GenAI applications across multiple AWS accounts. Leadership wants a unified dashboard showing token usage, costs, and error rates across all accounts.
questions.question: How should cross-account GenAI metrics be aggregated?

options.text: Manually export metrics from each account and combine in spreadsheets
Use CloudWatch cross-account observability to aggregate metrics to a central monitoring account
Create separate dashboards in each account and review them individually
Use AWS Cost Explorer for all cost-related metrics

questions.explanation: CloudWatch cross-account observability aggregates metrics from multiple accounts into a central monitoring account. You can create unified dashboards, alarms, and queries across all GenAI applications organization-wide.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual aggregation doesn't scale and doesn't provide real-time visibility.
incorrectExplanations.c: Separate dashboards don't provide the unified view requested. You'd need to switch between accounts.
incorrectExplanations.d: Cost Explorer shows costs but not operational metrics like error rates or token usage.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Cross-account metrics = CloudWatch cross-account observability

questions.examTip: CloudWatch cross-account observability enables unified monitoring across multiple AWS accounts.

questions.scenario: A document processing system needs to analyze 10,000 documents daily. Processing isn't time-sensitive—results are needed by the next business day. Each document requires about 5000 input tokens and generates 500 output tokens.
questions.question: Which invocation approach provides the LOWEST cost for this workload?

options.text: Process documents synchronously using Lambda with high concurrency
Use Bedrock batch inference to process documents asynchronously
Stream responses for each document for faster processing
Purchase Provisioned Throughput for consistent capacity

questions.explanation: Batch inference provides significant discounts (up to 50%) compared to on-demand pricing. Since results aren't time-sensitive, batch processing is ideal—submit the batch and retrieve results when processing completes.

incorrectExplanations.a: Synchronous processing uses on-demand pricing, which is more expensive than batch rates.
incorrectExplanations.c: Streaming affects perceived latency but doesn't reduce costs. Each token costs the same.
incorrectExplanations.d: Provisioned Throughput guarantees capacity but doesn't provide the batch discount for non-time-sensitive work.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Non-time-sensitive bulk = batch inference

questions.examTip: Batch inference provides up to 50% discount. Use it when processing time isn't critical.

questions.scenario: A company uses Claude 3 Sonnet for all GenAI tasks. Cost analysis shows that translation tasks account for 40% of costs but could be handled by a smaller model. Code generation tasks need the full capabilities of Sonnet.
questions.question: How should model selection be optimized for cost?

options.text: Continue using Sonnet for all tasks to simplify the architecture
Route translation tasks to Claude Haiku and keep code generation on Sonnet
Switch all tasks to Haiku for maximum savings
Fine-tune Haiku for code generation to handle all tasks

questions.explanation: Model routing sends tasks to appropriate capability tiers. Translation is well-handled by Haiku at lower cost. Code generation needs Sonnet's capabilities. This optimizes cost-to-capability ratio.

incorrectExplanations.a: Using Sonnet for translation wastes money on tasks that don't need its capabilities.
incorrectExplanations.c: Haiku can't match Sonnet's code generation quality. Quality would degrade for 60% of tasks.
incorrectExplanations.d: Fine-tuning Haiku for code doesn't elevate it to Sonnet's capability level for complex generation.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Task-specific cost optimization = route to appropriate model tier

questions.examTip: Match task complexity to model capability. Simple tasks go to cheaper models; complex tasks go to capable models.

questions.scenario: A GenAI agent orchestrates multiple tool calls during complex requests. The team needs to understand tool usage patterns: which tools are called most frequently, which combinations are common, and which tools cause delays.
questions.question: How should tool call patterns be monitored?

options.text: Review agent trace logs manually when issues occur
Publish custom CloudWatch metrics for each tool call with dimensions for tool name, duration, and success/failure
Enable CloudTrail for API call logging
Use Amazon Athena to query historical invocation data

questions.explanation: Custom CloudWatch metrics for tool calls enable real-time monitoring, dashboards, and alerting. Dimensions allow analysis by tool name, latency distribution, and success rates. This provides operational visibility into agent behavior.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual review is reactive and doesn't provide operational visibility or pattern analysis at scale.
incorrectExplanations.c: CloudTrail logs API calls but doesn't capture agent internal tool call patterns with the same detail.
incorrectExplanations.d: Athena for historical analysis is useful but doesn't provide real-time operational monitoring.

eliminationHints: CloudTrail = API level, not agent tools
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Agent tool observability = custom CloudWatch metrics with tool dimensions

questions.examTip: Custom metrics with dimensions enable agent tool observability. Track tool name, duration, and outcomes.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application experiences occasional spikes in response time. X-Ray traces show that most latency comes from the Bedrock API call, but standard CloudWatch Bedrock metrics don't show anomalies during these spikes.
questions.question: Which technique helps identify the cause of intermittent Bedrock latency spikes?

options.text: Increase Lambda timeout to accommodate longer responses
Correlate X-Ray traces with prompt characteristics (length, complexity) to identify patterns
Switch to a different model
Enable detailed CloudWatch monitoring

questions.explanation: Correlating traces with prompt characteristics reveals patterns—perhaps long prompts, certain topics, or specific input patterns cause slower responses. This identifies whether the issue is workload-dependent or infrastructure-related.

incorrectExplanations.a: Longer timeouts accommodate delays but don't identify causes. The root cause remains unknown.
incorrectExplanations.c: Model switching is a major change and doesn't address understanding the current behavior.
incorrectExplanations.d: Detailed CloudWatch monitoring at the metrics level doesn't capture prompt-level patterns.

eliminationHints: Model switch = major change, not diagnosis
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Latency diagnosis = correlate traces with workload characteristics

questions.examTip: Correlate traces with request characteristics to identify workload-dependent latency patterns.

questions.scenario: A customer service application has high cache hit rates for FAQ-type queries but low hit rates for personalized queries that include customer-specific context. The team wants to maximize caching effectiveness.
questions.question: Which TWO caching strategies improve cache utilization for this mix of query types?

options.text: Use semantic caching to match FAQ queries by meaning rather than exact text
Cache response templates for personalized queries and fill in customer data dynamically
Increase cache TTL to keep responses longer
Cache all responses regardless of query type

questions.explanation: Semantic caching improves FAQ hit rates by matching paraphrased questions. For personalized queries, caching response templates (with placeholders for customer data) enables cache hits while maintaining personalization.

incorrectExplanations.c: Longer TTL doesn't help if queries don't match. The issue is cache key matching, not expiration.
incorrectExplanations.d: Caching everything including highly personalized queries wastes cache space with low-hit entries.

parseStrategy.keyPhrase: maximize caching for mixed query types
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Mixed query caching = semantic for FAQs + templates for personalized

questions.examTip: Different query types need different caching strategies. Semantic for variations, templates for personalized.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application's traffic is unpredictable—it receives bursts of requests when featured on social media but low traffic otherwise. The team needs to ensure the system handles bursts without provisioning for peak capacity 24/7.
questions.question: Which configuration handles unpredictable traffic bursts cost-effectively?

options.text: Provision Lambda with high concurrency reserved permanently
Use on-demand Bedrock with Lambda auto-scaling and no reserved concurrency
Queue requests in SQS and process at a fixed rate
Purchase Provisioned Throughput for expected peak capacity

questions.explanation: On-demand Bedrock scales automatically with request volume. Lambda auto-scaling handles compute bursts. You pay only for what you use, with no commitment for quiet periods. This matches unpredictable traffic patterns.

incorrectExplanations.a: Reserved concurrency costs money during quiet periods. It's wasteful for unpredictable bursts.
incorrectExplanations.c: Fixed-rate processing can't handle bursts—requests queue up and users wait.
incorrectExplanations.d: Provisioned Throughput for peak wastes money during low-traffic periods.

eliminationHints: Provisioned peak = wastes during low traffic
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Unpredictable bursts = on-demand scaling with no reservation

questions.examTip: On-demand pricing with auto-scaling handles unpredictable traffic. Provisioned is for predictable sustained load.

questions.scenario: A company wants to be alerted when their GenAI application's daily token usage exceeds budget thresholds.
questions.question: How should token usage alerts be implemented?

options.text: Check usage manually in the AWS console daily
Create CloudWatch Alarms on token usage metrics with SNS notifications
Set up AWS Cost Anomaly Detection
Use AWS Budgets with cost thresholds

questions.explanation: CloudWatch Alarms can trigger on token usage metrics (custom or from Bedrock). Configure thresholds for daily limits and send SNS notifications when exceeded. This provides near-real-time alerting.

incorrectExplanations.a: Manual checking doesn't provide timely alerts and is easy to forget.
incorrectExplanations.c: Cost Anomaly Detection finds unusual spending patterns but doesn't alert on specific token thresholds.
incorrectExplanations.d: Budgets work on costs, not tokens directly. Token usage is more precise for this use case.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Usage threshold alerts = CloudWatch Alarms

questions.examTip: CloudWatch Alarms on token metrics provide immediate alerting for usage thresholds.

questions.scenario: A RAG application retrieves 10 documents and feeds them to the FM for answer generation. Analysis shows that the FM often ignores relevant documents at the end of the context, leading to incomplete answers.
questions.question: Which technique addresses this context utilization issue?

options.text: Retrieve fewer documents to shorten the context
Use reranking to order documents by relevance and place most relevant first
Use a model with a larger context window
Increase the temperature for more creative synthesis

questions.explanation: FMs exhibit 'lost in the middle' behavior—they pay more attention to content at the beginning and end of context. Reranking places the most relevant documents first, ensuring they receive attention. This improves answer completeness.

incorrectExplanations.a: Fewer documents might exclude relevant information. The issue is ordering, not quantity.
incorrectExplanations.c: A larger context window doesn't solve attention distribution. The model still tends to ignore middle content.
incorrectExplanations.d: Temperature affects creativity, not context utilization patterns.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Context utilization = reranking for optimal document ordering

questions.examTip: Reranking addresses 'lost in the middle' by placing important content where models pay attention.

questions.scenario: A development team wants to estimate costs before deploying their GenAI application. They need to understand how input length, output length, and request volume affect their bill.
questions.question: Which factors MOST significantly impact Bedrock API costs?

options.text: Request frequency and time of day
Input token count, output token count, and model selected
Lambda memory size and execution duration
Region and availability zone selection

questions.explanation: Bedrock charges per token for both input and output. Different models have different per-token rates. Total cost = (input tokens × input rate) + (output tokens × output rate) per model. These are the primary cost drivers.

incorrectExplanations.a: Bedrock doesn't have time-of-day pricing. Request frequency affects total but isn't a rate factor.
incorrectExplanations.c: Lambda costs are separate from Bedrock costs. Bedrock billing is token-based.
incorrectExplanations.d: Bedrock pricing is similar across regions for the same models. It's not a significant factor.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Bedrock cost = tokens × model rate

questions.examTip: Bedrock costs scale with tokens. Input and output are priced separately, and model selection affects rates.

questions.scenario: A company is implementing comprehensive observability for their production GenAI application. They want to track request flow, identify errors, and measure business outcomes.
questions.question: Which TWO AWS services provide complementary observability capabilities?

options.text: X-Ray for distributed tracing of request flow through services
CloudWatch for metrics, logs, and business-level dashboards
Config for resource configuration tracking
Inspector for security vulnerability assessment

questions.explanation: X-Ray traces individual requests across services, showing latency breakdown and errors. CloudWatch provides metrics, logs, and custom dashboards for business KPIs. Together, they offer comprehensive operational and business visibility.

incorrectExplanations.c: Config tracks resource configuration changes, not application observability.
incorrectExplanations.d: Inspector finds security vulnerabilities, not application performance issues.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Comprehensive observability = X-Ray (tracing) + CloudWatch (metrics/logs)

questions.examTip: X-Ray and CloudWatch are complementary: tracing for request flow, metrics/logs for aggregated visibility.

questions.scenario: A GenAI chatbot has a simple conversation pattern: short user questions (10-30 tokens) followed by concise AI responses (50-100 tokens). Users complain about waiting too long for responses.
questions.question: For this short-form interaction pattern, which invocation method provides the FASTEST response?

options.text: Synchronous InvokeModel for complete responses
Streaming InvokeModelWithResponseStream for progressive display
Batch inference for lower latency
Asynchronous processing with polling

questions.explanation: For short responses, synchronous InvokeModel is fastest. Streaming adds protocol overhead that isn't worthwhile for 50-100 token responses. The complete response returns nearly as fast as the first streaming chunk would.

incorrectExplanations.b: Streaming overhead isn't justified for short responses. The stream setup time negates any benefit.
incorrectExplanations.c: Batch inference is for non-time-sensitive workloads. It adds latency, not reduces it.
incorrectExplanations.d: Async with polling adds multiple round trips. It's slower than synchronous for short responses.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Short responses = synchronous fastest. Long responses = streaming for perceived latency.

questions.examTip: Synchronous is faster for short outputs. Streaming benefits long outputs where progressive display matters.

questions.scenario: A company's GenAI workload has grown predictably over 6 months. They now process 100,000 tokens per minute consistently during business hours. On-demand costs are significant, and they want to explore committed capacity options.
questions.question: Which analysis should the team perform before purchasing Provisioned Throughput?

options.text: Compare Provisioned Throughput hourly cost against current on-demand spend for the same period
Purchase the minimum Provisioned Throughput and scale up as needed
Wait for traffic to double before considering commitment
Switch to a cheaper model instead of provisioning capacity

questions.explanation: Cost comparison is essential. Calculate on-demand cost for your actual token volume, compare to Provisioned Throughput hourly rate for equivalent capacity, factor in usage patterns (business hours only), and determine break-even.

incorrectExplanations.b: Minimum provisioning without analysis might underprovision. You need to calculate required capacity from usage.
incorrectExplanations.c: Waiting doesn't help—current costs are the issue. Growth projections can factor into sizing.
incorrectExplanations.d: Model selection is separate from capacity pricing. You might need the current model's capabilities.

eliminationHints: Wait for growth = doesn't address current costs
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Provisioned Throughput decision = cost comparison with current usage

questions.examTip: Always compare Provisioned Throughput cost against actual on-demand spend before committing.

questions.scenario: A RAG application uses OpenSearch for vector storage. The operations team notices that retrieval latency has slowly increased over the past month despite consistent query patterns.
questions.question: Which factor most likely explains the gradual latency increase?

options.text: OpenSearch indexes require periodic optimization as documents are added
The embedding model is generating lower-quality vectors
Users are asking more complex questions
CloudWatch monitoring is adding overhead

questions.explanation: As documents are added to OpenSearch indexes, fragmentation increases. Index optimization (force merge, segment management) maintains performance. Without periodic maintenance, retrieval latency gradually increases.

incorrectExplanations.b: Embedding model behavior is consistent. It doesn't degrade over time with usage.
incorrectExplanations.c: The scenario states consistent query patterns—complexity hasn't changed.
incorrectExplanations.d: CloudWatch monitoring has minimal overhead and doesn't increase over time.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Gradual index degradation = fragmentation requiring optimization

questions.examTip: Vector indexes need periodic optimization. Monitor and maintain as document count grows.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application processes concurrent requests from multiple users. During peak usage, some users experience throttling errors while others get successful responses.
questions.question: How should the application handle Bedrock throttling during concurrent usage?

options.text: Increase Lambda reserved concurrency to match peak user count
Implement request queuing with exponential backoff retry for throttled requests
Reject requests when throttling is detected
Switch to a different model that has more capacity

questions.explanation: Request queuing absorbs bursts. Exponential backoff with jitter retries throttled requests without overwhelming the service. Users experience slightly longer waits during peaks rather than failures.

incorrectExplanations.a: Lambda concurrency doesn't affect Bedrock throttling limits. These are separate quotas.
incorrectExplanations.c: Rejecting requests provides poor user experience. Queuing with retry is more graceful.
incorrectExplanations.d: Different models may have similar throttling limits. This doesn't address the retry strategy.

eliminationHints: Lambda concurrency = doesn't affect Bedrock limits
parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Throttling handling = queue + exponential backoff retry

questions.examTip: Queue requests and implement exponential backoff for graceful throttling handling.

questions.scenario: A developer is optimizing their prompt to reduce token usage. Their current prompt includes detailed instructions repeated in every request.
questions.question: Which prompt optimization technique reduces token costs?

options.text: Move repeated instructions to the system prompt and use prompt caching
Use longer, more detailed instructions for better clarity
Include more examples in each request
Repeat important instructions twice for emphasis

questions.explanation: System prompts with prompt caching are processed once and reused. This avoids paying for the same instruction tokens on every request. Structure prompts to maximize cacheable content.

incorrectExplanations.b: Longer instructions mean more tokens and higher costs.
incorrectExplanations.c: More examples add tokens per request. Use few-shot learning sparingly.
incorrectExplanations.d: Repetition doubles token count for those instructions without proportional benefit.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Token reduction = minimize per-request tokens, maximize cached content

questions.examTip: Move static content to system prompts with caching. Minimize variable per-request content.

questions.scenario: A GenAI application's quality has degraded, but standard metrics (latency, error rate) are unchanged. The team suspects subtle prompt regression but has no baseline comparison.
questions.question: Which technique helps establish and maintain quality baselines for future regression detection?

options.text: Create golden datasets with expected outputs and run evaluations regularly
Increase error logging verbosity
Add more CloudWatch metrics
Survey users about perceived quality

questions.explanation: Golden datasets with expected outputs establish quality baselines. Regular evaluation runs detect when outputs drift from expectations. This catches quality regression that doesn't appear in operational metrics.

incorrectExplanations.b: Verbose logging captures what happened but doesn't establish quality baselines or detect drift.
incorrectExplanations.c: CloudWatch metrics capture operational data, not output quality assessments.
incorrectExplanations.d: User surveys are subjective and delayed. They can't detect specific regressions systematically.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Quality baselines = golden datasets with regular evaluation

questions.examTip: Golden datasets establish quality baselines. Run evaluations regularly to catch regression early.

questions.scenario: A content moderation AI needs to make consistent decisions for similar content. Randomness in outputs causes users to receive different moderation decisions for essentially identical posts.
questions.question: Which parameter adjustment ensures consistent moderation decisions?

options.text: Set temperature to 0 for deterministic outputs
Increase top_p for broader token selection
Use streaming for faster decisions
Increase max_tokens for more detailed reasoning

questions.explanation: Temperature 0 makes token selection deterministic—the model always selects the highest probability token. This ensures identical inputs produce identical outputs, critical for consistent moderation.

incorrectExplanations.b: Higher top_p increases variety by considering more token candidates. This makes outputs less consistent.
incorrectExplanations.c: Streaming affects delivery, not decision consistency.
incorrectExplanations.d: max_tokens affects length, not decision determinism.

parseStrategy.decisionFramework: Consistent outputs = temperature 0

questions.examTip: Temperature 0 produces deterministic outputs. Use it when consistency matters more than variety.
